scenario: miss edwards is here for evaluation of facial pain this is a 54 -year-old male
patient_utterances: miss edwards is here for evaluation of facial pain this is a 54 -year-old male
[Clinician asked: "how're you doing doctor cruz nice to see you today"] good to see you mister edwards i'm doctor

[Clinician asked: "tell me what's been going on yeah so i've got this stabbing shooting pain i've had for a while you know and it it especially right here in my right face right in that cheek bone area it's sometimes it gets super hot i've had it for i think a couple of months now and i went to my family doctor and th"] okay great i would like to ask you a little bit more about that

[Clinician asked: "had in your face do you remember how long that you had it probably for about two or three months just just came on slowly i started noticing it but now it's i have episodes where just shooting stabbing kind of a pain in my right cheek bone right face area"] mm-hmm do you have any sensory loss meaning like numbness or tingling in that part of your face

[Clinician asked: "not that i've noticed no okay do you have any symptoms like this anywhere else on your face including the other side of your face"] no it's just on the right side mostly not on the left side just on the right side of my face

[Clinician asked: "have you noticed any weakness on that side of the face like when you smile or while you're doing other things any weakness there not really i mean i've i've i've tried to you know when i smile my smile seems equal on both sides my eyes i do n't have any weakness there my vision seems to be unchanged"] sometimes if i'm if certain temperatures seem to trigger it sometimes or if it's super where it's cold i get a trigger sometimes

[Clinician asked: "sometimes certain kind of sensory outside of the wind sometimes that seems to trigger it but that's about it"] okay and anything that you've done to to that helps for a little bit when you

[Clinician asked: "pain excess"] you know i've tried ibuprofen and motrin that had really has n't helped it just comes on suddenly and then it's kinda stabbing excruciating pain i've tried rubbing some you know some tiger balm on it that did n't work
[Clinician asked: "but that's you know so i i went to my family doctor and he said you know i really need to see a neurosurgeon"] got it how long do these episodes last these pain

[Clinician asked: "you know it can last for anywhere from a few minutes to sometimes about an hour but generally generally a few minutes"] any history of something like multiple sclerosis or any brain tumors that you know

[Clinician asked: "no nothing like that alright any other kinda headache symptoms that have anything like you had migraines or anything related to the headaches i mean i get occasional headaches but not really i do n't have a history of migraines but i occasionally get headaches like everybody else i take some tylenol"] how severe is the pain on a scale of one to ten

[Clinician asked: "when it comes on it's like a ten it's like somebody is stabbing you with an ice pick and but usually you know after a few minutes usually it goes away but sometimes it can last up to an hour great so mister i would like to do a physical exam if that's okay with you"] sure

[Clinician asked: "i would like you to follow my finger here and i see that you're following my finger in in both directions can you show me your teeth"] that looks nice and symmetric i'm gon na rub my fingers next to your ear can you hear that
[Clinician asked: "yep"] you can hear it on the other side as well
[Clinician asked: "yep"] okay
[Clinician asked: "i'm gon na take this cotton tape and run it along the side of your face can you feel that okay"] yeah it's a little bit numb on my right side not so much on my left side

[Clinician asked: "okay alright i'm gon na use this little needle here and i'm gon na poke here and i wan na see if you feel like it's being sharp or dull on that part of your face does that feel different or normal"] it feels a little bit dull on my left on my right side my left side it feels sharp
[Clinician asked: "alright good well i had a chance to look at your mri"] okay

[Clinician asked: "and i looked at your mri and it appears to have small blood vessel that is abutting and perhaps even pinching the trigeminal nerve the trigeminal nerve is nerve that comes from the brainstem that goes out to the face and provides the sensory inflammation from the face and you may have a condition ca"] okay

[Clinician asked: "where the nerve compression causes this kind of shooting electrical pain in the face how do we treat it"] well the first line would be to try some medications usually we start with medications that are called gabapentin
[Clinician asked: "or tegretol these are medications that really help reduce the excitability of the nerve"] okay

[Clinician asked: "most people can get the pain control with that but there are some people where the medications are n't gon na be enough and in that situation we would consider surgery i would n't i would n't recommend that now we usually try the medications first"] for considering a surgery to decompress the nerve the root cause of the problem is the compression of the blood vessel against the nerve

[Clinician asked: "okay so we should be tried which one would you recommend the tegretol or yeah i think we could start with the tegretol to start with i just want to make sure that you understand some of the potential side effects that you can have with this"] sure

[Clinician asked: "it's always a little bit of trial and error to figure out what the right dosing that would work for you but some common side effects can include you know memory loss tingling imbalance some people can actually have like dermatologic"] skin reaction to this medication and particularly people who have eustachian descent so that we may do some genetic testing just to make sure that it will be safe for you

[Clinician asked: "okay sounds good let's do it"] okay so i will prescribe that for you and then we will see how that goes and if your pain continues we can talk about different surgical options to treat the pain

[Clinician asked: "yeah i think i would like to try the tegretol first and if that does n't work then i can come back and so once should i come back and just to kinda check back with you and see if you know if it's had enough time for a fact or not"] i think one month would be a great time to follow up

[Clinician asked: "okay sounds good so if you want to send that prescription over to my pharmacy that would be fine and then why do n't i come back in about a month and we'll go from there"] great i'll see you then
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right-sided facial pain.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Eyes: Denies vision changes or weakness in right eye.
HENT: Reports right sided facial pain. Denies left sided facial pain. Neurological: Reports headaches. Denies sensory loss, numbness, or tingling in the right cheek. Denies right sided facial weakness.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Neurological
- Orientation: Light touch testing of the right side of the face reveals numbness when compared to the contralateral side. Pinprick testing is sharp on the left side of the face; dull on the right side of the face.

Ears, Nose, Mouth, and Throat
Bilateral finger rub test is negative.
- Examination of Mouth: Teeth are symmetric.

RESULTS

MRI of the head is reviewed today. This demonstrates a small blood vessel that is abutting, and perhaps even pinching, the right trigeminal nerve.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Trigeminal neuralgia.
- Medical Reasoning: The patient has been experiencing facial pain for 2 to 3 months. A recent MRI revealed a small blood vessel abutting, or perhaps even pinching, the right trigeminal nerve. The root cause of his facial pain is the compression of the blood vessel against this nerve.
- Patient Education and Counseling: The patient and I discussed treatment options including medicating with gabapentin or Tegretol. I explained the common side effects associated with these medications can include memory loss, tingling, and imbalance. We discussed the need for genetic testing due to the possible side effect of dermatologic reactions in people of East Asian descent. We also briefly discussed surgical treatment to decompress the nerve, but this is not recommended at this time.
- Medical Treatment: A prescription for Tegretol will be sent to the patient’s pharmacy.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 1 month. If at that time his facial pain persists, we may further discuss surgical options.

naive.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your symptoms with me, Mr. Edwards. I understand how frustrating and debilitating this facial pain must be for you. Based on your description and the MRI results, it seems like you're experiencing trigeminal neuralgia, which is caused by a blood vessel pressing on the trigeminal nerve. This can lead to the intense, shooting pain you're describing.

We'll start with Tegretol to help manage the nerve-related pain. It's important to take it as prescribed and let me know if you experience any side effects. If the medication doesn't provide relief within a month, we can explore other options, including surgery. Please come back for a follow-up in about four weeks, and we'll reassess your progress. I'm here to support you through this, and we'll work together to find the best solution for your pain.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me. It sounds like the facial pain you're experiencing has been quite distressing, especially with those sudden stabbing episodes. I can understand how this must interfere with your daily life and activities.

Based on your description and the MRI findings, it seems the pain might be related to trigeminal neuralgia, which often occurs when a blood vessel presses on the trigeminal nerve. This can cause intense, shooting pains, especially in the cheek area, and is a common cause of such symptoms. The numbness you noticed on the right side during the exam also aligns with this possibility.

I appreciate that you've already tried over-the-counter medications like ibuprofen and tiger balm without relief. Now, let's focus on a treatment plan. The first step is usually medications like gabapentin or Tegretol, which help calm nerve activity. Studies show these can be effective for many people [PMID ?]. If these don't help, we might consider surgical options to relieve the nerve compression.

We'll start with the medication and schedule a follow-up in about four weeks to assess your progress. In the meantime, keep track of your symptoms and any changes. Before you leave, could you summarize our plan in your own words to ensure you understand it clearly?

Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or concerns. I'm here to support you through this.

It sounds like you're dealing with some facial pain that's been going on for a while now. I want to make sure I understand how this is affecting you.

The kind of pain you're describing — shooting, stabbing in your right cheek bone — can be tricky to pinpoint, but it might be related to things like nerve issues or infections. Since you've already tried several self-care steps without full relief, it's important we get a clearer picture.

We'll need to do some tests to figure out what's causing this pain. This might include imaging or a neurological exam to look for possible causes.
